content.text: Every ARM deployment runs in one of two modes: Incremental or Complete. The mode determines how ARM reconciles the template against the current state of the resource group — specifically, what happens to resources that exist in the group but are not declared in the template.

Incremental mode (the default) is additive: resources in the template are created or updated, and resources absent from the template are left unchanged. Complete mode enforces the template as the exact desired state: resources not declared in the template are deleted from the resource group.

attrs.alt: Incremental vs Complete mode: incremental leaves unlisted resources intact; complete deletes them

content.text: Incremental mode is safe for iterative development. You can deploy a template that covers only a subset of your resource group, or add resources progressively, without worrying about removing things you have not declared yet. It is the mode used by default when --mode is omitted.

# Incremental mode (default) — add/update only, never delete
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --mode Incremental  # optional: this is the default

# Scenario: RG contains VM-A, Storage-B, NSG-C
# Template declares VM-A and Storage-B only
# Result: VM-A updated, Storage-B updated, NSG-C left intact

# PowerShell
New-AzResourceGroupDeployment \
  -ResourceGroupName myapp-rg \
  -TemplateFile main.bicep \
  -Mode Incremental
Resources in the template: created if new, updated if they exist with different properties
Resources in the RG but not in the template: left completely untouched
Safe to run multiple times (idempotent for the resources it manages)
Suitable for modular deployments where multiple teams own different parts of a resource group

Complete mode makes the template authoritative. After deployment, the resource group's contents exactly match what the template declares. Any resource in the group that is not in the template — regardless of how it was created — is deleted. This makes Complete mode a powerful tool for enforcing a clean desired state, but dangerous if the template is incomplete.

# Complete mode — template IS the desired state; unlisted resources are deleted
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --mode Complete

# Scenario: RG contains VM-A, Storage-B, NSG-C
# Template declares VM-A and Storage-B only
# Result: VM-A updated, Storage-B updated, NSG-C DELETED

# PowerShell
New-AzResourceGroupDeployment \
  -ResourceGroupName myapp-rg \
  -TemplateFile main.bicep \
  -Mode Complete
Always run --what-if before executing a Complete mode deployment in production. The what-if output explicitly lists every resource that will be deleted with a '-' prefix.
The what-if operation previews exactly what ARM would create, update, or delete without making any changes. For Complete mode, pay attention to every '-' (delete) line in the output.

# Always run --what-if before Complete mode to preview deletions
az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --mode Complete

# Example output:
# Resource and property changes are indicated with these symbols:
#   ~ Modify
#   + Create
#   - Delete
#
#   - Microsoft.Network/networkSecurityGroups/nsg-myapp-prod-eus2
#   ~ Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-myapp-prod-eus2
#     ~ properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize: 'Standard_D2s_v3' => 'Standard_D4s_v5'
#   + Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/stmyappprodeus2

# Only proceed after reviewing all '-' (delete) entries
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --mode Complete

# PowerShell what-if
New-AzResourceGroupDeployment \
  -ResourceGroupName myapp-rg \
  -TemplateFile main.bicep \
  -Mode Complete \
  -WhatIf

The deployment mode is not a property of the Bicep template file — it is a parameter passed at deployment time. The same template can be deployed in Incremental mode during development and Complete mode in a production pipeline.

Complete mode is only valid for resource group scope deployments. Subscription-scope and management-group-scope deployments only support Incremental mode. Nested child deployments can independently specify their own mode.

# Complete mode only applies to RESOURCE GROUP scope deployments.
# Subscription-scope and management-group-scope deployments are always Incremental.

# This works (resource group scope):
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --mode Complete

# This does NOT support Complete mode (subscription scope):
az deployment sub create \
  --location eastus2 \
  --template-file subscription.bicep
  # mode defaults to Incremental; specifying Complete would be an error

# Nested deployments: Complete mode in a child deployment
# A nested deployment (Microsoft.Resources/deployments) can specify
# its own mode independent of the parent:
# {
#   'type': 'Microsoft.Resources/deployments',
#   'properties': {
#     'mode': 'Complete',
#     'template': { ... }
#   }
# }

Extension resources (role assignments, locks, diagnostic settings, Azure Policy assignments) are not managed by Complete mode deletions. ARM preserves them even when their parent or sibling resources are deleted. Locks on a resource that IS deleted will also be removed along with the resource.

# Extension resources (locks, role assignments, diagnostic settings)
# are NOT deleted by Complete mode deployments.
# ARM treats them separately from 'regular' resources.

# Example: Complete mode deployment where RG has:
# - vm-myapp (in template)
# - nsg-myapp (NOT in template)
# - lock on nsg-myapp (extension resource on nsg-myapp)
# - role assignment on RG (extension resource)

# Result of Complete deployment:
# - vm-myapp: updated (in template)
# - nsg-myapp: DELETED (not in template)
# - lock on nsg-myapp: DELETED (ARM deletes its parent)
# - role assignment on RG: KEPT (extension resources survive Complete mode)

# Verify that locks and role assignments are preserved:
az lock list --resource-group myapp-rg -o table
az role assignment list --resource-group myapp-rg -o table

Terraform always uses Complete-style semantics for the resources it manages: removing a resource from a .tf file causes it to be destroyed on next apply. However, Terraform's scope is limited to what it tracks in state — resources created outside of Terraform are invisible to it and will not be deleted.

# Terraform always operates in 'Complete' semantics:
# resources declared in .tf files define the desired state;
# resources removed from .tf files are destroyed on next apply.

# This is similar to ARM Complete mode, but scoped to what Terraform manages.
# Resources created outside Terraform (manually, via CLI) are NOT tracked
# and will NOT be deleted by terraform apply.

# To see what Terraform would delete:
terraform plan

# To apply and delete removed resources:
terraform apply

# Key difference from ARM Complete mode:
# ARM Complete deletes ALL resources in the RG not in the template,
# including ones Terraform doesn't know about.
# Terraform only deletes resources it tracks in its state file.
Resources in template / .tf: exist in RG
Resources in template / .tf: new to RG
Resources NOT in template / .tf: in RG

Every ARM deployment is recorded in deployment history, scoped to the target resource group, subscription, management group, or tenant. History is capped at 800 deployments; ARM automatically prunes older records when the limit is approached.

# List the last 10 deployments for a resource group
az deployment group list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query '[].{name:name,state:properties.provisioningState,ts:properties.timestamp}' \
  -o table

# Get the template used for a specific deployment
az deployment group export \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name 2024-01-15-prod-deploy

Because ARM stores each deployment's template, you can redeploy an earlier known-good snapshot to recover from a bad change. This is especially useful after a failed Complete-mode deployment that deleted resources unintentionally.

# Export the last-known-good template
az deployment group export \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name last-known-good \
  > recovered-template.json

# Redeploy it (Incremental mode by default)
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name rollback-$(date +%Y%m%d) \
  --template-file recovered-template.json \
  --mode Incremental
Tip: Keep a naming convention like prod-YYYYMMDD-NNN for deployments so history is auditable and rollback targets are easy to identify.

For long-running deployments, ARM exposes individual resource-level operation records. Each operation has a provisioning state, start/end time, status code, and error detail. This is especially valuable for Complete-mode deployments where many resources may be deleted or re-created in sequence.

DEPLOYMENT=my-complete-deploy
RG=myRG

# Stream operation-level status while a deployment runs
while true; do
  az deployment operation group list \
    --resource-group "$RG" \
    --name "$DEPLOYMENT" \
    --query '[].{resource:properties.targetResource.resourceName,state:properties.provisioningState}' \
    -o table
  STATE=$(az deployment group show -g "$RG" -n "$DEPLOYMENT" \
    --query properties.provisioningState -o tsv)
  [ "$STATE" != "Running" ] && break
  sleep 10
done
echo "Final state: $STATE"

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

ops = client.deployment_operations.list(
    resource_group_name=rg_name,
    deployment_name=deployment_name,
)

for op in ops:
    props = op.properties
    target = props.target_resource
    print(
        f"{target.resource_name:<40} "
        f"{props.provisioning_state:<15} "
        f"{props.status_code}"
    )
    if props.status_message and props.status_message.error:
        print(f"  ERROR: {props.status_message.error.message}")
Safe CI/CD Patterns with Complete Mode
Complete mode's delete-on-omit behavior makes it powerful for enforcing IaC compliance, but a deploy-time mistake can cause unintended data loss. A --what-if gate in CI/CD prevents surprises.
GitHub Actions Pipeline with What-If Gate

name: Deploy Infrastructure
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  whatif:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          creds: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CREDENTIALS }}

      - name: What-If Preview
        run: |
          az deployment group what-if \
            --resource-group ${{ vars.RG }} \
            --template-file infra/main.bicep \
            --mode Complete \
            --result-format FullResourcePayloads 2>&1 | tee whatif.txt
          # Fail if any deletions are detected
          grep -q 'Delete' whatif.txt && \
            echo '::error::Complete mode will DELETE resources — review diff' && exit 1 || true

  deploy:
    needs: whatif
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          creds: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CREDENTIALS }}

      - name: Deploy (Complete Mode)
        run: |
          az deployment group create \
            --resource-group ${{ vars.RG }} \
            --template-file infra/main.bicep \
            --mode Complete \
            --name deploy-${{ github.run_number }}
The what-if job blocks the deploy job if deletions are detected. A required reviewer approval in the production environment adds a human gate before the actual deployment executes.

Complete mode applies only at the top-level deployment scope. Nested deployments — created with the Microsoft.Resources/deployments resource type inside a parent template — always run in Incremental mode regardless of the parent's mode setting.

Even if the parent template runs in Complete mode, the nested deployment only adds or updates the storage account — it never deletes resources in the resource group that are absent from the nested template.
Resources already processed may be deleted
Use Incremental for most scenarios: it is safe by default, supports all scopes, and does not risk unintended deletions.
Use Complete only when you need strict IaC compliance and are willing to treat every non-template resource as drift to be removed.
Always run --what-if before a Complete-mode deployment in production.
Never use Complete mode on resource groups that contain resources managed by other teams or pipelines.
Bicep modules and linked templates always run as Incremental, even inside a Complete-mode parent deployment.

Incremental mode (the default) is safe for iterative and modular deployments — it only creates and updates template-declared resources and leaves everything else alone. Complete mode makes the template authoritative for the entire resource group, deleting anything not declared. Always use --what-if before a Complete deployment, and restrict Complete mode to environments where the template genuinely covers everything in the resource group.
